title: 22.	Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock with Cooldown.
description: 22.	Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock with Cooldown.

examples.explanation: Day 0 (price=1, state=can_buy): Option1 - buy: profit = -1 + recurse(day1, state=can_sell). Option2 - cooldown: profit = 0 + recurse(day1, state=can_buy). Choose max. Day 1 (price=2, state=can_sell from Option1): Option1 - sell: profit = 2 + recurse(day2, state=cooldown). Option2 - hold: profit = 0 + recurse(day2, state=can_sell). Choose max. Day 2 (price=3, state=cooldown from Option1): Must cooldown: profit = 0 + recurse(day3, state=can_buy). Day 3 (price=0, state=can_buy): Option1 - buy: profit = 0 + recurse(day4, state=can_sell). Option2 - cooldown: profit = 0 + recurse(day4, state=can_buy). Choose max. Day 4 (price=2, state=can_sell from Option1): Option1 - sell: profit = 2 + recurse(day5, state=cooldown). Option2 - hold: profit = 0 + recurse(day5, state=can_sell). Choose max. Day 5: Base case, return 0. Backtracking: Best sequence: Buy at day0 (price1), Sell at day1 (price2) profit=1, Cooldown day2, Buy at day3 (price0), Sell at day4 (price2) profit=2, Total=3.

Day 0 (price=1, state=can_buy): Option1 - buy: profit = -1 + recurse(day1, state=can_sell). Option2 - cooldown: profit = 0 + recurse(day1, state=can_buy). Day 1: Base case, return 0. So buy option gives -1, cooldown gives 0. Choose max = 0. No transactions possible in single day.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Explore all possible sequences of buying, selling, and cooldown days using recursion, checking every combination to maximize profit. This approach is straightforward but impractical for large inputs due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Stock Trading with Cooldown
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all buy/sell/cooldown sequences to maximize profit.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force recursive solution to the 'Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown' problem. The intuition is to explore every possible sequence of actions (buy, sell, or cooldown) on each day, respecting the constraints: you can only buy if you don't own stock, you can only sell if you own stock, and after selling you must take a cooldown day. The algorithm uses recursion with memoization to avoid redundant calculations. At each day, we have three possible states: 'can_buy' (don't own stock and can buy), 'can_sell' (own stock and can sell), and 'cooldown' (just sold, must wait). The base case is when we reach the end of prices array. We recursively explore both buying (if in 'can_buy' state) and selling (if in 'can_sell' state) options, tracking maximum profit. The time complexity is O(2^n) because at each step we can branch into two possibilities (buy or not, sell or not), though memoization reduces some redundancy. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and memoization table. This approach connects to the full problem by providing a complete but inefficient solution that forms the foundation for optimized approaches. Common mistakes include forgetting the cooldown constraint, not handling state transitions correctly, and missing base cases. Real code pattern uses recursion with state parameter and memoization dictionary.

examples.trace: Day 0 (price=1, state=can_buy): Option1 - buy: profit = -1 + recurse(day1, state=can_sell). Option2 - cooldown: profit = 0 + recurse(day1, state=can_buy). Choose max. Day 1 (price=2, state=can_sell from Option1): Option1 - sell: profit = 2 + recurse(day2, state=cooldown). Option2 - hold: profit = 0 + recurse(day2, state=can_sell). Choose max. Day 2 (price=3, state=cooldown from Option1): Must cooldown: profit = 0 + recurse(day3, state=can_buy). Day 3 (price=0, state=can_buy): Option1 - buy: profit = 0 + recurse(day4, state=can_sell). Option2 - cooldown: profit = 0 + recurse(day4, state=can_buy). Choose max. Day 4 (price=2, state=can_sell from Option1): Option1 - sell: profit = 2 + recurse(day5, state=cooldown). Option2 - hold: profit = 0 + recurse(day5, state=can_sell). Choose max. Day 5: Base case, return 0. Backtracking: Best sequence: Buy at day0 (price1), Sell at day1 (price2) profit=1, Cooldown day2, Buy at day3 (price0), Sell at day4 (price2) profit=2, Total=3.

Day 0 (price=1, state=can_buy): Option1 - buy: profit = -1 + recurse(day1, state=can_sell). Option2 - cooldown: profit = 0 + recurse(day1, state=can_buy). Day 1: Base case, return 0. So buy option gives -1, cooldown gives 0. Choose max = 0. No transactions possible in single day.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with State Tracking
ladders.desc: Core recursive function that explores buy/sell decisions based on current state.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: a recursive function that takes current day index and state as parameters, and returns maximum profit achievable from that point. The intuition is that profit maximization depends not just on prices but on whether we currently own stock (can_sell state) or not (can_buy state), plus cooldown state after selling. The algorithm: function dfs(day, state): if day >= n: return 0 (base case). If memo[day][state] exists: return it. If state == can_buy: profit1 = dfs(day+1, can_sell) - prices[day] (buy option). profit2 = dfs(day+1, can_buy) (cooldown option). result = max(profit1, profit2). If state == can_sell: profit1 = dfs(day+2, can_buy) + prices[day] (sell option, note day+2 for cooldown). profit2 = dfs(day+1, can_sell) (hold option). result = max(profit1, profit2). If state == cooldown: result = dfs(day+1, can_buy) (must wait). Memoize and return result. Time complexity for this function is O(2^n) without memoization, O(n) with memoization for each state. Space O(n) for stack and memo. This connects to L0 by being the main recursive engine. Common mistakes: incorrect state transitions (e.g., not jumping to day+2 after sell), wrong profit calculations, missing memoization. Real pattern: recursive DFS with state parameter.

examples.trace: dfs(0, can_buy): Option1 - buy: profit = dfs(1, can_sell) - 1. dfs(1, can_sell): Option1 - sell: profit = dfs(3, can_buy) + 2 = 0+2=2. Option2 - hold: profit = dfs(2, can_sell) = 0. max=2. So buy option gives 2-1=1. Option2 - cooldown: profit = dfs(1, can_buy). dfs(1, can_buy): Option1 - buy: profit = dfs(2, can_sell)-2 = 0-2=-2. Option2 - cooldown: profit = dfs(2, can_buy)=0. max=0. So cooldown option gives 0. Overall max=1.

dfs(0, can_sell): We own stock bought earlier. Option1 - sell: profit = dfs(2, can_buy) + 3 = 0+3=3. Option2 - hold: profit = dfs(1, can_sell). dfs(1, can_sell): Option1 - sell: profit = dfs(3, can_buy)+2 = 0+2=2. Option2 - hold: profit = dfs(2, can_sell)=0. max=2. So hold option gives 2. Overall max = max(3,2)=3. But since we started with can_sell, we assume stock was bought at some cost. If bought at price >=3, profit could be 0 or negative. For example if bought at 3, profit=0. So output depends on initial conditions.

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Machine for Stock Trading Decisions
ladders.desc: Modeling the problem as state transitions between can_buy, can_sell, and cooldown states.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is modeling the trading process as a state machine with three states: can_buy (don't own stock, eligible to buy), can_sell (own stock, eligible to sell), and cooldown (just sold, must wait one day). The intuition is that at any day, your possible actions and future profits depend entirely on which state you're in, not just the prices. State transitions: From can_buy: if buy → go to can_sell (and subtract price from profit), if cooldown → stay in can_buy. From can_sell: if sell → go to cooldown (and add price to profit), if hold → stay in can_sell. From cooldown: must wait → go to can_buy. This state machine captures all constraints: can't buy if own stock, can't sell if don't own stock, must cooldown after selling. The algorithm uses this state model to structure decisions. Time/space analysis: The state machine has 3 states, leading to O(n) possible state sequences. Connection to L1: This state model defines the parameters for the recursive function. Common mistakes: Confusing states, incorrect transitions (e.g., from sell to can_buy without cooldown), not resetting profit correctly on transitions. Real pattern: Represent states as integers or enums, define transition rules.

examples.input: Sequence: can_buy → buy → can_sell → sell → cooldown → can_buy
examples.trace: Start: state=can_buy, profit=0. Day0: Buy at price p0: state→can_sell, profit = -p0. Day1: Sell at price p1: state→cooldown, profit = -p0 + p1. Day2: Cooldown: state→can_buy, profit unchanged. This sequence shows one complete trade cycle.

examples.input: Sequence: can_buy → cooldown → can_buy → buy → can_sell → hold → can_sell
examples.trace: Start: can_buy. Day0: Cooldown: stay can_buy. Day1: Buy: go to can_sell. Day2: Hold: stay can_sell. This shows delaying purchase and then holding stock.

testCases.input: Start: can_buy, action: buy then sell next day, prices=[3,7]
Start: can_sell, action: sell then wait, price=6
Start: can_buy, action: cooldown then buy, prices=[2,4]
Start: can_sell, action: hold then sell, prices=[5,8]
Start: can_buy, action: buy, hold, sell, prices=[1,2,3]

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Decision Making with Two Choices
ladders.desc: Implementing recursive exploration of two options (action vs no-action) at each step.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the recursive pattern of making a decision between two options at each step, which is fundamental to the brute force approach. The intuition is that at many points in the algorithm, we face a binary choice: for example, when in 'can_buy' state, we choose between buying or not buying (cooldown). When in 'can_sell' state, we choose between selling or holding. The algorithm: function explore(day, option1_profit, option2_profit): if base_case: return 0. profit1 = option1_profit + explore(next_day1, ...). profit2 = option2_profit + explore(next_day2, ...). return max(profit1, profit2). This pattern repeats throughout the solution. Time complexity without memoization is O(2^n) because each decision branches into two possibilities. Space O(n) for recursion depth. Connection to L2: This decision-making implements the state transitions. Common mistakes: Not exploring both branches, incorrect profit accumulation, missing base case. Real pattern: Recursive function that calls itself twice for two options and takes max.

examples.input: Simple choice: Buy or not at price 5, then future profit 3 if buy, 1 if not
examples.trace: Option1 - buy: profit = -5 + 3 = -2. Option2 - not buy: profit = 0 + 1 = 1. max = 1. So choose not to buy.

examples.input: Sell or hold: current profit if sell=10, if hold leads to future profit 12
examples.trace: Option1 - sell: profit = 10. Option2 - hold: profit = 12. max = 12. So choose to hold.

Sell: gain=7, future=0; Hold: gain=0, future=8
Buy: cost=10, future=15; Not buy: cost=0, future=6
Sell: gain=20, future=0; Hold: gain=0, future=25

Sell: gain=5, future=0; Hold: gain=0, future=3

Sell: gain=9, future=0; Hold: gain=0, future=10
Buy: cost=8, future=12; Not buy: cost=0, future=5

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Profit Calculation for a Single Trade
ladders.desc: Computing profit from buying at one price and selling at a later price.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental operation: calculating profit from a single buy-sell transaction. The intuition is simple: profit = selling price - buying price. However, in the context of the full problem, this operation is embedded within larger sequences and state management. The algorithm: profit = sell_price - buy_price. If profit is negative, it represents a loss. This operation is used whenever we execute a 'buy' action (subtracting price from total profit) and a 'sell' action (adding price to total profit). Time O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: This calculation is part of the decision-making for each option. Common mistakes: Subtracting when should add, confusing profit with revenue, not handling negative profits correctly. Real pattern: Simple arithmetic subtraction and addition.

examples.trace: profit = 15 - 10 = 5
profit = 18 - 20 = -2 (loss)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Accessing elements in an array using indices, fundamental for processing price sequences.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how to work with arrays (or lists) and access elements by index. The intuition is that stock prices are given as an array where each element represents the price on a specific day, and we need to process them in sequence. The algorithm: Given an array prices of length n, we can access price on day i as prices[i] where i ranges from 0 to n-1. We iterate through days using a loop or recursion with index parameter. Time O(1) for access, O(n) for traversal. Space O(1) for index variable. Connection to L4: We use array indexing to get buy and sell prices for profit calculation. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds (accessing beyond n-1), off-by-one errors, confusing day numbers with indices. Real pattern: Use for-loop or while-loop with index variable.

examples.trace: prices[1] = 20, since indexing starts at 0.
i=0: prices[0]=5, i=1: prices[1]=4, i=2: prices[2]=3, i=3: prices[3]=2.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with State Machines O(n)
approaches.summary: Model the problem as a state machine with three states: hold, sold, and rest, using dynamic programming to track maximum profit at each day. This efficiently handles the cooldown constraint by transitioning between states based on allowed actions.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) depending on implementation

ladders.title: Full Problem — Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock with Cooldown using DP State Machine
ladders.desc: Complete solution using dynamic programming with three states to maximize profit with cooldown constraint.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum profit from buying and selling stocks with a one-day cooldown after selling before buying again. The state machine approach models three states: HOLD (owning stock), SOLD (just sold stock), and REST (not owning after cooldown). At each day, we update these states based on allowed transitions: from HOLD, you can HOLD or SELL; from SOLD, you must go to REST; from REST, you can REST or BUY. The intuition is that these states capture all possible positions while enforcing the cooldown. The algorithm initializes HOLD to -prices[0] (buying on day 0), SOLD to 0, and REST to 0. For each price from day 1, compute new states: newHOLD = max(oldHOLD, oldREST - price) (hold or buy from rest), newSOLD = oldHOLD + price (sell from hold), newREST = max(oldREST, oldSOLD) (rest or cool down from sold). After processing all days, the answer is max(SOLD, REST). Time complexity is O(n) as we iterate through prices once. Space complexity is O(1) if we use variables, or O(n) if we store arrays. This connects to L1 by implementing the full state transitions. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization or missing the cooldown in transitions. Real code pattern: Initialize hold, sold, rest; loop through prices; update variables; return max(sold, rest).

examples.trace: Day 0: price=1, hold=-1, sold=0, rest=0. Day 1: price=2, newHold=max(-1, 0-2)=-1, newSOLD=-1+2=1, newREST=max(0,0)=0; update: hold=-1, sold=1, rest=0. Day 2: price=3, newHold=max(-1, 0-3)=-1, newSOLD=-1+3=2, newREST=max(0,1)=1; hold=-1, sold=2, rest=1. Day 3: price=0, newHold=max(-1, 1-0)=1, newSOLD=-1+0=-1, newREST=max(1,2)=2; hold=1, sold=-1, rest=2. Day 4: price=2, newHold=max(1, 2-2)=1, newSOLD=1+2=3, newREST=max(2,-1)=2; hold=1, sold=3, rest=2. Result: max(3,2)=3.
Day 0: price=1, hold=-1, sold=0, rest=0. No more days. Result: max(0,0)=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — State Transition Updates for DP
ladders.desc: Implement the core update logic for the three states at each day.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on updating the HOLD, SOLD, and REST states for a given price, based on previous states. The intuition is that these updates enforce the cooldown constraint: you can only buy from REST (after cooldown), and selling moves you to SOLD, which then forces a transition to REST. The algorithm takes previous hold, sold, rest, and current price as inputs. Compute newHold = max(prevHold, prevRest - price): either keep holding or buy from rest (since buying requires not having sold the previous day). newSOLD = prevHold + price: sell the stock you hold. newREST = max(prevRest, prevSold): either stay in rest or enter rest from sold (cooldown). Return the new states. Time complexity is O(1) per update. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the iterative step in the full solution. Common mistakes include swapping prevRest and prevSold in calculations or missing the max operation. Real code pattern: def update(hold, sold, rest, price): newHold = max(hold, rest - price); newSOLD = hold + price; newREST = max(rest, sold); return newHold, newSOLD, newREST.

examples.trace: newHold = max(-1, 0-2) = max(-1, -2) = -1. newSOLD = -1 + 2 = 1. newREST = max(0, 0) = 0.
newHold = max(2, 1-5) = max(2, -4) = 2. newSOLD = 2 + 5 = 7. newREST = max(1, 3) = 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Modeling States for Cooldown Constraint
ladders.desc: Define the three states and allowed transitions to handle cooldown.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the state machine model: HOLD (owning stock), SOLD (just sold), and REST (not owning, eligible to buy). The intuition is that these states capture all possible scenarios while enforcing that after selling, you must wait one day (cooldown) before buying again. Transitions: From HOLD, you can HOLD (do nothing) or SELL (move to SOLD). From SOLD, you must go to REST (cooldown). From REST, you can REST (do nothing) or BUY (move to HOLD). This ensures no consecutive buy-sell without a break. The algorithm involves defining these states as variables representing maximum profit in that state. Time and space are O(1) for the model itself. This connects to L1 by providing the basis for update rules. Common mistakes include adding extra states or incorrect transitions. Real code pattern: States are integers hold, sold, rest; transitions as described.

examples.input: Start in REST with $0, price=10, action=BUY
examples.trace: From REST, buying at price 10: spend $10, profit becomes -10, state changes to HOLD.

examples.input: Start in HOLD with -$5, price=15, action=SELL
examples.trace: From HOLD, selling at price 15: profit = -5 + 15 = 10, state changes to SOLD.

ladders.title: Building Block — Maximum Profit Update with Previous States
ladders.desc: Compute new profit values using max of previous states.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves calculating new profit values for states based on previous ones, using the max operation to choose the best option. The intuition is that at any day, you want the maximum possible profit in each state, considering all allowed actions. For example, for HOLD, profit = max(previous HOLD profit, previous REST profit - current price), because you can either continue holding or buy from rest. Similarly, REST profit = max(previous REST, previous SOLD) as you can stay resting or cool down from sold. This ensures optimal decisions. The algorithm takes previous profit values and current price, applies these formulas. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L2 by implementing the profit calculations for transitions. Common mistakes include using min instead of max or incorrect arithmetic. Real code pattern: newHoldProfit = max(oldHoldProfit, oldRestProfit - price); newRestProfit = max(oldRestProfit, oldSoldProfit).

examples.trace: newHoldProfit = max(-1, 0-2) = max(-1, -2) = -1.
newRestProfit = max(3, 5) = 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Initialize DP States for Stock Problem
ladders.desc: Set initial profit values for HOLD, SOLD, and REST states.
ladders.explanation: This operation initializes the three state variables before processing prices. The intuition is that on day 0, you can either buy the stock (enter HOLD) or do nothing (stay in REST). SOLD is not possible on day 0 since you can't sell without owning. Thus, initialize HOLD to -prices[0] (profit if you buy on day 0), SOLD to 0 (since no sale), and REST to 0 (profit if you do nothing). This sets up the base for iterative updates. The algorithm takes the first price as input and returns initial hold, sold, rest. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L3 by providing starting values for profit updates. Common mistakes include initializing HOLD to 0 or SOLD to negative. Real code pattern: hold = -price; sold = 0; rest = 0.

examples.trace: Buy on day 0: spend 5, so hold profit = -5. No sale possible, sold=0. Do nothing, rest=0.
Buy on day 0: spend 0, so hold profit = 0. sold=0, rest=0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Profit Calculation in Stock Trading
ladders.desc: Basic idea of profit as selling price minus buying price.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept explains that in stock trading, profit is calculated as selling price minus buying price. If you buy a stock at price B and sell at price S, your profit is S - B. This can be negative if you sell at a loss. In sequences, you sum profits from multiple trades. The intuition is that to maximize profit, you aim to buy low and sell high. For a single trade, profit is straightforward; for multiple trades with constraints like cooldown, it becomes more complex. The algorithm for this basic operation is simply profit = sellPrice - buyPrice. Time and space are O(1). This connects to L4 by being the fundamental calculation used in state profits. Common mistakes include subtracting in wrong order or misunderstanding profit signs. Real code pattern: def profit(buyPrice, sellPrice): return sellPrice - buyPrice.

examples.trace: Profit = 15 - 10 = 5.
Profit = 5 - 20 = -15 (loss).

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Memoization O(n)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to cache results for subproblems defined by day and holding status, reducing overlapping computations. This top-down approach simplifies implementation while maintaining optimal time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock with Cooldown using DP with Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to find maximum profit with cooldown constraint.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum profit from buying and selling stocks with a cooldown period after selling. The key insight is that at any day, you can be in one of three states: holding a stock, not holding and in cooldown, or not holding and ready to buy. We use a top-down DP approach with memoization to avoid recomputation. The recursive function takes two parameters: the current day index and a state indicating whether we're holding a stock. The base case is when we reach beyond the last day, returning 0 profit. For each day, if holding, we can either sell (move to cooldown) or skip (continue holding). If not holding, we can either buy (move to holding) or skip (stay not holding). The cooldown is enforced by ensuring that after selling, we skip the next day before buying again. Memoization caches results for (day, holding) pairs to achieve O(n) time complexity. The intuition is that we're exploring all possible sequences of buy/sell/skip decisions while respecting constraints, and caching ensures we don't recompute overlapping subproblems. This approach simplifies implementation by breaking the problem into smaller decisions at each day. Time complexity is O(n) because each state is computed once, and space complexity is O(n) for the memoization cache and recursion stack.

examples.trace: Day 0 (price 1), not holding: Option 1 - buy (profit -1, move to holding), Option 2 - skip (profit 0, stay not holding). Choose buy. Day 1 (price 2), holding: Option 1 - sell (profit +2, total 1, move to cooldown), Option 2 - skip (profit 0, stay holding). Choose sell. Day 2 (price 3), cooldown: Must skip (profit 0). Day 3 (price 0), not holding: Option 1 - buy (profit -0, total 1, move to holding), Option 2 - skip (profit 0, stay not holding). Choose buy. Day 4 (price 2), holding: Option 1 - sell (profit +2, total 3), Option 2 - skip (profit 0). Choose sell. Total profit 3.
Day 0 (price 1), not holding: Option 1 - buy (profit -1, but no future day to sell, so net loss), Option 2 - skip (profit 0). Choose skip. Total profit 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with State Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement the core recursive function that decides actions based on holding state.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the solution: a recursive function that, given a day index and a boolean indicating whether we're currently holding a stock, returns the maximum profit achievable from that state onward. The function explores two main branches: if holding, we can sell (adding current price to profit and moving to a cooldown state) or skip (moving to next day still holding). If not holding, we can buy (subtracting current price from profit and moving to holding) or skip (moving to next day still not holding). The cooldown is handled by ensuring that after selling, we skip the next day before considering buying again—this is implemented by advancing the day index by 2 when we sell. The base case is when the day index is out of bounds, returning 0. The intuition is that at each step, we make a locally optimal decision that contributes to the global maximum, and recursion allows us to explore all possibilities. This sub-routine directly feeds into the full problem by being called from the top-level function with initial parameters (day 0, not holding). Time complexity for this sub-routine without memoization is exponential, but with memoization it becomes O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack.

examples.trace: Day 0, not holding: Option 1 - buy: profit = -1 + recurse(day=1, holding=True). Recurse day 1, holding: Option 1 - sell: profit = +2, total 1, day out of bounds after skip, return 1. Option 2 - skip: profit = 0 + recurse(day=2, holding=True) returns 0, total -1. So buy gives 1. Option 2 - skip: profit = 0 + recurse(day=1, holding=False). Recurse day 1, not holding: Option 1 - buy: profit = -2 + recurse(day=2, holding=True) returns 0, total -2. Option 2 - skip: profit = 0, total 0. So skip gives 0. Max is 1 from buy.

Day 0, holding: Option 1 - sell: profit = +3, then skip day 1 due to cooldown, recurse(day=2, holding=False) returns 0, total 3. Option 2 - skip: profit = 0 + recurse(day=1, holding=True). Recurse day 1, holding: Option 1 - sell: profit = +2, total 2, then skip to day 3 returns 0, total 2. Option 2 - skip: profit = 0, total 0. So skip gives max 2. Compare: sell gives 3, skip gives 2, max is 3? Wait, but if sell at day 0, profit 3, then cooldown day 1, cannot buy, so total 3. If skip at day 0, then sell at day 1 gives 2, total 2. So max is 3. Correction: output should be 3. Let's recompute: Day 0, holding: sell -> profit 3, move to day 2 (skip day 1), recurse day 2 returns 0, total 3. skip -> profit 0 + recurse(day=1, holding=True). Day 1, holding: sell -> profit 2, move to day 3, returns 0, total 2; skip -> 0, total 0. So max is 3. I apologize, the trace had an error. Correct output is 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Transitions with Cooldown Constraint
ladders.desc: Define the state machine and transitions for buy, sell, and skip actions.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves modeling the problem as a state machine with three states: holding a stock (H), not holding and in cooldown (C), and not holding and ready to buy (R). However, for memoization, we simplify to two states: holding (True) and not holding (False), with cooldown embedded in transitions. From holding state, we can transition to not holding by selling (which forces a cooldown, so next state is not holding but with a skipped day), or stay holding by skipping. From not holding state, we can transition to holding by buying, or stay not holding by skipping. The cooldown is enforced by ensuring that after a sell action, we skip the next day before any buy action—this is implemented by advancing the day index by 2 when we sell, instead of 1. The intuition is that each day, we decide an action based on current state, and the profit accumulates accordingly. This state transition logic is the foundation of the recursive function. It connects to the key sub-routine by providing the rules for how to update day index and profit. Time and space analysis for this logic alone is O(1) per transition, but in context, it drives the O(n) overall complexity. Common mistakes include forgetting the cooldown skip or mishandling state transitions.

examples.input: State: holding=True, action=sell, current price=5, next prices=[2,3]
examples.output: Transition to not holding, profit +5, skip next day
examples.trace: Start holding stock. Action sell: add current price 5 to profit. Due to cooldown, must skip next day (price 2). So move to day after next (price 3) in not holding state. Profit increment is +5.

examples.input: State: holding=False, action=buy, current price=3, next prices=[4]
examples.output: Transition to holding, profit -3, move to next day
examples.trace: Start not holding. Action buy: subtract current price 3 from profit. Move to next day (price 4) in holding state. Profit increment is -3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Memoization Cache for State Results
ladders.desc: Implement a cache to store computed results for (day, holding) pairs.
ladders.explanation: Memoization is a technique to store the results of expensive function calls and return the cached result when the same inputs occur again. In this problem, we use a dictionary or array to cache the maximum profit for a given day index and holding state. The key is a tuple (day, holding), and the value is the profit. Before computing recursively for a state, we check if it's already in the cache; if so, return it directly. Otherwise, compute the result, store it in the cache, and then return it. This reduces the time complexity from exponential to O(n) because each unique state is computed only once. The intuition is that many recursive calls overlap—e.g., from different paths, we might reach the same day and holding state—so caching avoids redundant work. This building block feeds into the core logic by making the recursive function efficient. Time complexity for cache operations is O(1) on average, and space complexity is O(n) for storing up to 2n states. Common mistakes include using mutable keys or not invalidating cache correctly, but here, days and holding are immutable.

examples.input: Cache empty, compute for day=2, holding=True, result=5
examples.trace: Start with empty cache {}. Compute profit for day 2, holding=True, get result 5. Store key (2, True) with value 5 in cache. Cache becomes {(2, True): 5}.

examples.input: Cache has (2, True): 5, request for day=2, holding=True
examples.trace: Cache is {(2, True): 5}. Request profit for day 2, holding=True. Check cache, found key (2, True) with value 5. Return 5 without recomputation.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Day Index Manipulation with Bounds Check
ladders.desc: Handle advancing day index and checking if it's within array bounds.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves incrementing the day index by 1 or 2 based on the action (skip or sell) and checking if the new index is within the bounds of the prices array. If the index is out of bounds (i.e., greater than or equal to the length of the array), it means no more days are available, and the profit from that point onward is 0. This is crucial for the base case of recursion. The operation is simple: given a current day index i and a skip amount k (1 for skip/buy, 2 for sell due to cooldown), compute next_i = i + k. Then, check if next_i < len(prices); if not, return a sentinel value indicating end of recursion. The intuition is that we need to stop exploring when there are no more days, as no further profit can be made. This feeds into the building block by providing the termination condition for recursive calls. Time and space complexity are O(1). Common mistakes include off-by-one errors or mishandling of cooldown skip.

examples.trace: Current day index is 2. Skip amount is 1. Compute next day = 2 + 1 = 3. Check if 3 < 4 (length of prices). Yes, so next day is within bounds.
Current day index is 3. Skip amount is 2. Compute next day = 3 + 2 = 5. Check if 5 < 4. No, so next day is out of bounds.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion with Base Case
ladders.desc: Learn how recursion works with a simple example and base case termination.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. It consists of two parts: the base case, which stops the recursion by returning a simple value without further calls, and the recursive case, which breaks the problem down and calls the function again with modified parameters. In this context, we use recursion to explore decisions at each day: the function calls itself for future days after making a decision (buy, sell, or skip). The base case is when the day index is out of bounds, meaning no more days to consider, so profit is 0. The intuition is that by recursively exploring all paths, we can find the maximum profit. This foundation feeds into the basic operation by providing the framework for how day index manipulation leads to recursive calls. Time complexity without optimization can be exponential, but with memoization it becomes efficient. Space complexity depends on recursion depth. Common mistakes include missing base case or infinite recursion.

examples.input: Simple recursion: sum of numbers from 1 to n
examples.trace: Define function sum(n): if n <= 0, return 0 (base case). Else, return n + sum(n-1) (recursive case). For n=3: sum(3) calls 3 + sum(2). sum(2) calls 2 + sum(1). sum(1) calls 1 + sum(0). sum(0) returns 0. Then back: sum(1) returns 1+0=1, sum(2) returns 2+1=3, sum(3) returns 3+3=6.

examples.input: Recursion with condition: find max in list
examples.output: max = max(current, recursive max of rest)
examples.trace: Define function max_list(arr, index): if index >= len(arr), return -infinity (base case). Else, return max(arr[index], max_list(arr, index+1)). For arr=[2,5,1], index=0: max(2, max_list([2,5,1],1)). max_list at index=1: max(5, max_list([2,5,1],2)). max_list at index=2: max(1, max_list([2,5,1],3)). max_list at index=3 returns -infinity. Back: index=2 returns max(1, -inf)=1, index=1 returns max(5,1)=5, index=0 returns max(2,5)=5.
